Hey, I'm Peter.

And today, I'm going to

To show you how

It is indeed, as anyone

A malicious actor can

So you should never, ever

The best way to protect

The good news is that
Firebase has built a solution

to help secure your app
from these malicious actors.

When you use the Firebase AI
SDK to access generative models

like Gemini, you don't put
a Gemini API key directly
in your app.

Instead, the key
stays on the server

and can only be accessed

This is achieved by using

and make sure it only
serves requests that

multimodal input to

in the photo of the

augmented generation to

from the database,

tool used to call an API to

Let's think about the

Sure, we could run

But let's be honest.

In this scenario, this might
not be the ideal solution,

as you'd have to coordinate

and transfer potentially a

between your client
app and those services.

By moving this complex
flow to the backend,

we drastically reduce the amount
of data being transferred.

This translates to faster
response times, a better user

experience, and less strain
on the user's device,
especially on spotty networks.

And this is where
Genkit comes in.

Genkit is Google's open-source
AI integration framework.

It allows you to implement
AI flows using TypeScript.

And we've just announced
support for Go and Python.

Genkit flows are like
regular functions,

which means you can do
anything that you would also

be able to do in
any other function,

like calling other APIs,
retrieving data from a database,

performing
computations, et cetera.

But the cool thing is that
Genkit flows are observable.

And that means you can monitor
them using the AI Monitoring

dashboard in the
Firebase console

or any other tool that supports
OpenTelemetry, such as Google
Cloud's Observability Suite.

Genkit also provides
easy-to-use abstractions

for working with models
and vector stores.

And this enables
us to tap into any

of the recipes our users have
stored in their app's database

and use them to generate new
recipes based on the ingredients
the user has on hand.

Another really cool
feature of Genkit
is its unified model interface.

This allows you to
use different models

by just providing the
name of the model you
want to use instead.

This makes it super
easy to experiment
with different models.

Let's take a look at how
you would implement the meal
planning use case with Genkit.
First, we create a Genkit flow.

Then we pass the user's
input to the model,

prompting it to detect
which ingredients
can be seen in the picture.

By providing an
output schema, we

can tell Genkit to
work with the model

to return structured output,

Now that we have the
list of ingredients,

we need to find

also fit the user's needs,
things dietary restrictions
and preferred cuisines.

That's no easy task when dealing
with complex search queries.

Retrieving relevant
data from a database

based on free-form inputs,
such as natural language

or a list of ingredients,
is a common challenge
in AI applications.

That's where Genkit's retriever
abstractions comes in.

It streamlines this
complex process,

making it surprisingly
efficient.

You can think of the retriever
as your intelligent search
assistant.

It takes the user's
request, translates it

into a vector
embedding, and then

uses nearest-neighbor search to
quickly locate the best matching
recipes in your database.

Vector embeddings
are essentially

a way to turn text into
a series of numbers
that a computer can understand.

Here is the number
representation for apples.

And here is the number
representation for oranges.

Since these are vectors, we
can use mathematical functions

like cosine to measure
how similar they are.

This allows our
system to understand

that apples and oranges are
both fruits, even though they
are different.

So it turns out you can
compare apples and oranges.

Our recipes are stored
in Cloud Firestore, which
supports vector embeddings.

So when the user
stores a new recipe,

we also calculate vector
embeddings for its ingredients
and store them alongside it.

Genkit's Firestore
retriever can then

use those embeddings to quickly
find the recipes that best match

a user's ingredients, like those
detected in their fridge photo.

The result is a number
of Firestore documents

that contain the best
matching recipes.

Using these recipes,
we prompt the model

to generate a brand-new
recipe for the specified

number of people,
taking inspiration

from the recipes
found in the database.

This flow does a lot with
a few lines of code thanks

This is a really valuable

You don't have to add any

It's all built in.
And there you have it.

We've implemented a bunch
of exciting new features

for our app, making
use of the AI services
available from Firebase.

We use the Firebase AI SDK to
call Gemini to generate recipes
based on the user's input.

We used both text input

allowing our users to
generate recipes based
on what's in their fridge.

We then used multimodal

images showing

We were able to implement all of

using the same
tools and languages

that we're used to

We then switched it

Genkit to implement
a feature that

grounds the model in the user's

to provide results

And finally, we made our
application ready for production

and looked at how Firebase's
AI monitoring features can

help us operate the AI features
of our app with confidence.

So now you might be wondering
when you should use the Firebase
AI SDKs versus Genkit.

The answer is, it
depends on your use case.

If you're implementing
features that

don't require you to tap
into a large data set or data

of any other backend
services, the Firebase AI SDKs

are a great way to add powerful
AI features to your app.

As you saw, it's
easy to get started,

and you have access to a wide
spectrum of generative AI
features.

On the other hand, if
your use case requires

you to tap into
a large data set,

for example, a product
database, or if you

need to connect your model
to other backend services,
Genkit is the best choice.

It allows you to
implement AI flows that

integrate all those
various services

and make them accessible to your
client application via callable
API endpoints.

Genkit is also the
go-to way if you'd

like to integrate non-Google
models, like those
from Anthropic or OpenAI.

Finally, keep in mind that this
isn't an either/or decision.

As you saw in our
example, you can

have the best of both worlds
by using the Firebase AI SDKs
and Genkit in the same app.
Pick the best tool for the job.

Thanks for watching, and I
will see you in the next one.
